transcript.text: Aida, did you say we want to review the images today?
Yeah.
Do you have that pipeline pulled up?
I can go and find it.
I do.
Okay, great.
Thank you.
I know we're working with some, like, really good designers, but every time they create a pipeline, I'm always so impressed by how good the images look.

Like, um, like, I think, like, I'm just, like, remembering what it used to be like when we're still on Aeroops, and basically we would hack together, like Daniel would hack together, if they wanted stock imagery with, like, a color overlay, they would have to use, like, an API, and it was always just a struggle to get it to, like, a good spot, and then, like, right now it's just so much, so much better.
Yeah.
We also got Otto's ImagedInPyPlan up and running.
Nice.
Hello.
Bye.
Hey, Bethany.
Dave is on the way.
He is running to another conference room.
Mm-hmm.
All right.
Then before I forget, Bethany, I am trying to find those examples for you.
Thank you.
Just passing it through a couple team members to see which ones are best to show.
No worries.
Yeah, we are kind of in planning mode for Q4 and then thinking about next year.
So I'm trying to figure out from a scale perspective, like, where we're going to get to.
So hence all of the questions.
Yep.
Totally understand.
I can't believe it's almost going to be, you know, we're in Q4 already.
Totally.
And Q4 doesn't
It to me.
It basically feels like nothing because of December being a sort of a fake month.
So I always say that to my team.
I'm like, just assume December is a garbage dump and barely anything's going to happen because of all the time off and blah, blah, blah.
And we're going to have to jam through October, November.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We always notice.
So there's a huge like drop in organic traffic from the end of November, kind of almost all the way through December and early January, which is, yeah, it's always funny to see.
Funny.
Stressful.
Depending on your role in a company.
Yes.
My, it will be stressful for us, I think.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Hi, Dave.
You're on mute.
Sorry.
There we go.
Sorry about that.
I had to run between floors here.
So sorry for being.
No worries.
Yeah, thank you guys for joining.
Before we begin, Bethany, we haven't actually met, so I'm an editorial account strategist at Growthx.
I work with Erik to develop, like, your content strategy, set you up in our content generation platform, and just kind of act as, like, the quality control.
And Bailey here, she's a new team member.
She's a managing editor, and she's going to be editing your content on a day-to-day basis.
Bailey, want to do a quick intro as well?
Yeah, hi.
It's really nice to meet you both.
I'm excited to be here.
My background is I've been working in content on marketing teams for mostly SaaS-based companies for the last 10 years, so companies like Greenhouse.
And most recently at an AI services company.
So I'm very excited to be here, and I'll be sort of editing your content, making sure the voice and the writing guidelines continue to get updated.
We get more comments from you.
So our results keep getting better and better.
So feel free to always reach out if you have questions or concerns.
I am here to address them.
Awesome.
Well, quickly, I'm Bethany.
I'm the director of brand.
I'll probably be in some of these calls and some not.
But we'll definitely be kind of keeping track of how things are going on Slack and on the dashboards.
Yeah.
And I'm Dave, Senior Content Marketing Manager here.
So I'll be more the day-to-day contact, the one kind of more digging into the content and the numbers itself.
Great.
Great to meet you both.
I think you're muted, Aida.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Got it.
Thanks.
So, Bailey, you want to do a quick content update?
We've revised the calibration blog, and we're also working on the three new blogs this week.
Yeah.
So I think we've revised the calibration blog.
I addressed the comments that I saw.
Bye-bye.
Thank
And that you left there.
So that one should be fully updated.
And we also just finished the first of the blogs we're delivering this week.
So you should have gotten an email with a link to a Google Doc asking you to take a look at that.
So same process, you can leave your comments directly in the doc, and we'll use those to update the article itself.

And then also feel free to, like, if you want to write a separate section, if you sort of have overall comments about voice or tone or approach or angle or anything like that, it's super helpful always, but especially, you know, at this early stage, because we'll use that to update the company context, the writing guidelines, anything kind of brand related as well.
So you can put all of that directly in the doc.
And then we also have two more posts that should be coming by the end of the week as well for you to review.
Amazing.
A quick question, just something that came to mind as I was reviewing the second version of the post.
And I don't know if this is a worthwhile concern, but I like I know.
Like as we kind of tried to lean a little more into the brand voice, that ends up in some cases changing what we're putting into like H2 and H3 headers.
Does that have a potential to negatively impact our performance from like an SEO and LLMO perspective?

I would say, yeah, like so whenever we update that, especially with the voice and tone guidelines, I think especially with yours, we updated it to sort of have that more conversational tone and it definitely went too far.
And so then we kind of pulled it back a little, which is part of what we're calibrating, right?
But we always at the end take a pass through to make sure that everything is still optimized for SEO and for, you know, AI search as well.
Especially when it comes to, you know, all the meta tags and the titles and all that.
So that's always kind of the final step to make sure that none of the changes we've made are going
And negatively affect how the content performs.
Perfect.
Sounds great.
Yeah.
And I think also from like, the most important thing is that the content is comprehensive.
So the tone can be different, you know, from, not from blog to blog, we don't want to do that.
But for example, like multiple companies can have like different tones, but they'll still be ranking pretty high for similar keywords.
So the key is really comprehensiveness and writing very clear, direct H2s and H3s.
And also making sure that each section can work as a standalone section on its own.
So you don't really need, like, you don't need additional information from other parts of the article to understand what another section is talking about.
So that's kind of like, it's a chunking technique, which LLMs use chunking to analyze and like understand information.
So by doing that, we're kind of like...
Like, already preparing the content for the LLMs because that's kind of how they, what they use to process information.
So, yeah, that's, that's always, it's always on our mind.
But when it comes to the tone, yeah, it does go, like, take some time to, like, kind of reel the tone in.
I think that AI tends to kind of go too far in one direction, but it's always easier, I think, to dial it back rather than to ramp it up.
Cool.
And then just really quickly, I wanted to give you just, like, a quick reminder because I know Bethany wasn't on the call when I was walking you through your Airtable.
So, Bethany, you should have access to the Content OS.
You can find a link to the Content OS in our shared space.
So, if you go to Docs and then you go to Content OS, there's a link right here.
I spent a bunch of time in
Okay, nice.
Perfect.
Yeah.
So if you're just curious about like what's in production, these two are in production right now.
And we have a couple that are approved to start.
Because we are ramping to five next week, we would love to get like additional content approved.
If there aren't any articles that you think are good in this batch of like 30 plus, just let us know, like probably like by the end of this week.
So we have enough time to do additional keyword research and prepare additional topics for you.
I want to dive into that just a tiny bit, because I'd love to get, you know, I can look at that and I can see like, yeah, there's lots here that I can see us writing about.
But I do want to make sure that we're also prioritizing the stuff that's actually going to move the needle.

Wondering if you can kind of, you know, help guide me on what I should be prioritizing potentially, like, should I be keeping everything like a good balance of the different clusters that we have identified?
Should I just be focusing on like, you know, the difficulty?
What's my best approach here?
All right.
So I think we want to have a good balance.
So the clusters, ideally, we would have almost like equal amounts from each cluster.
What I would pay attention to is the volume and the keyword difficulty for each article.
And we would like to have a balance of blogs that are kind of low volume, like pending expenses.
For example, it's like super low volume, but the keyword difficulty is very low as well.
So we're pretty much guaranteed to rank for this keyword.
And we also want to combine that with like more higher difficulty, like higher difficulty keywords, like maybe, yeah, pending transactions or account reconciliation.
This is pretty, it's not super difficult, but it is harder to rank for this.
But the search volume payoff is also greater.
So I think that's what we want to balance.
want balance because our- Okay.
So strategy does target like multiple clusters and we want to establish a presence across all of them.
Cool.
Okay.
Nice.
And I also wanted to show you the featured images that our designer created.
So we sent over all your materials and she came back with some examples.
So this is what they look like right now.
So we have like five different options.
This is what it looks like.
So it's like an AI generated human image with a text overlay and like a little bit of text below.
This is sorry.
So is this Dave, this for just like the, the images that would live on our blog?
it is.
Okay.
I think I would, um, I'd rather have our design team do these.
And because we have a specific style on the blog, and especially we're about to do a migration of the blog in the next couple of days.
So what I might say is maybe we'll go back as a team and figure out how to funnel this through.

But yeah, I think we're going to have to have the design team involved just because it's, I know it's going to be a thing about volume, but we want to make sure we have consistency with some of them already.
Yeah, so in terms of publishing, I think this is, I mean, waiting for the design team to generate images is probably going to slow us down when it comes to publishing.
And like, do you feel comfortable us just pushing an article live, maybe without an image or just using this image as like a stand-in?
And then you're going back in and like replacing it once the design team.
I'm fine with having stand-in images.
Our CMS, when we update in the next couple of days, we won't allow for no image on a post.
So I would say, yeah, stand-ins, but definitely we'll want to, Dave, circle with Carolyn and Polly and just see if we can figure out a funnel for these.
Yep.
Okay.
Yeah.
I mean, that works too.
I just, I remember when I joined GrowthX almost a year ago, we were working with a client that had their designers create the featured images and they were great.
Like they were really unique and creative.
But at one point we had like a backlog of 15 blogs that weren't published and we're just kind of sitting waiting to be published.
So I just want to like make sure that we can get that traffic, you know, right away.
Okay.
Yep.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
All right, perfect.
And then I think the last thing we've prepared for you this week is your Looker dashboard.
So I've given everyone access here, so you should be able to access it just from this link.
There's also a link in performance reports if you go here, but it's a multiple, it's like a multi-page report that breaks down data from your Google Analytics and Google Search Console.
So because you have editing access, you'll be able to kind of like change the data here.
For example, if you want to see just like referral traffic or paid search traffic, you can check these boxes.
And the first graph, this is just data from your Google Analytics account, and it's showing a breakdown between sessions versus engaged sessions.
This is one of the highest sessions versus engaged sessions ratios I've ever seen.
So this is like...
Really good.

I'm not sure if there's like a tracking thing that could be happening, but an engaged session in Google Analytics is basically a user visit that lasts longer than 10 seconds, includes a page scroll, or it includes a click on an internal link somewhere on your website.
So that's what Google counts as engagement.
It's basically the opposite of bounce rate.
And this is really important for SEO because Google does measure engagement to see if a web page is valuable and helpful.
And the longer the engagement, the better it is for your website overall.
Because like, if it's helpful to users, Google is going to rank it higher.
Yep.
And then, yeah, if you scroll down, you can see landing page performance.
This is just data from your GA4 account.
And you can also see a breakdown of sessions by different referral sources.
This is just all like Google, Bing.
Influencer Partnerships, everything that you have set up in GA4 is going to be pulled here.
I mean, you can also see a query ranking distribution.
This is data from your Google Search Console.
So you can see how many keywords you rank for that are in the top 10, top 20, and below.
The next page contains a breakdown of your non-branded traffic.
So basically, traffic from keywords that don't include your brand name.
And this is data from the Google Search Console.
So it includes just impressions and clicks.
And this is just data from Google.
So it doesn't include traffic from other search engines or any other sources.
Below that, we have the performance of different landing pages that rank for non-branded keywords.
This is also data from Google Search Console.
And there's a week-by-week breakdown.
For conversion events, so this gives you a breakdown of all of the different events that you already have set up in your GA4.
So I see that you have a couple of here already.
You can let us know which one, if you're tracking anything in particular here that we should pay special attention to.
I think there's just file download, event, form start, that's probably important.
The thing is a lot of times these events have not very specific names, so it's kind of hard to see what they're referring to.
But whoever set them up in GA4 can probably help out.
And if you add additional conversion events, just let us know because we might have to update the Looker dashboard to reflect the changes.
The cohorts page is particularly important to us because once we start publishing, we're going to be seeing a comparison of our content versus yours.
So right now we can just see non-growthx URLs, but it's going to start including our content's performance as well.
And in this table right here, we're going to be able to see the performance of individual clusters.

So we have four or five clusters that we're targeting, and we are able, because we link Looker to Airtable, we can measure the performance of individual clusters, and also just individual URLs, which are also going to populate here.
And this is data from your Google Search Console account.
This is data from your Google Search Console account.
It's kind of a блиrofx So these

It mimics the organic search report that you get when you log into the platform, and it shows you just a breakdown of, like, clicks, impressions, click-through rates, and average position over time, and it gives you kind of a breakdown of all the landing pages, including, like, the different click-through rates and the average positions for these web pages in Google search.
And then the next, last page are just different queries that you rank for.
It's taking a little while to load, but basically, queries are keywords in Google Search Console, so it measures, you know, all the keywords that you're ranking for.
This report is also helpful for topic ideation, so sometimes you might be ranking for a keyword that's really important, perhaps very high intent, and we might not even be aware that it exists.
Yes, we might not have.

I it yet, but you might have a web page that's still kind of ranking for it somewhere, so we can use and like by analyzing, for example, this table, we might come across like an informational keyword that is really interesting, like an interesting blog post idea, and because you're already kind of ranking for it, creating a blog post for that keyword would pretty much guarantee you page one rankings, so we can also use this report, not just for like performance measurement, but also for ideation.

And then the last page is just a breakdown of different LLM referral traffic, let me just give it a second to load, so CHAGBT, all right, so yeah, it gives you, if you look at the chart, so it gives you a breakdown of the traffic that's coming to your site from different LLMs, obviously CHAGBT is kind of leading here, but you're also getting traffic from Perplexity, Google Gemini, Copilot,
Pilot, Claude, Poe, and a couple of others.
And then if you go down to Referral Breakdown, it gives you like specific numbers from Google Analytics 4.
And the engagement rate on all of these is quite high.
So close to 100 on a lot of these.
And then if you scroll down, you can actually see the landing pages that are getting the most traffic from LLM.
So in this year so far, your homepage got the most sessions, which is very usual.
It's very like most of our clients have the most traffic from LLMs go to their homepage.
But for example, you have blog posts that are showing up in LLMs as well.
The not set thing, it's just a very frequent issue in Google Analytics tracking.
So it usually happens when someone has maybe an ad blocker.
On their phone or on their desktop computer, or, you know, there's some privacy measure.
They might not have accepted the cookies.
So basically, Google's tags just couldn't record the session.
But so I just, yeah, I wanted to clarify that.
Do you have any questions about the report or anything else?
I don't think so.
I'll spend some time with it also and just see if any questions come up.
Nice.
Okay, perfect.
All right, we're going to, I mean, the Looker performance report, it updates in near real time.
It depends when Google Search Console data is updated, but it should be pretty regularly updated automatically.
When we start publishing content, we're going to upload the list of published URLs to the Looker report.
So you can just see the performance here whenever you need access to that information.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And yeah, I think that's everything that I had to discuss today.
I don't know if anybody else wanted to discuss anything else.
How long after you start publishing, would you typically start seeing some signals and some results coming in from this company?
For like the initial, I mean, it usually takes a week to start seeing like impressions, sometimes even clicks.
And then the more we publish, the more it compounds.
So I think it's usually around like the two-month mark that you really start to see like an impact on the site in terms of keyword rankings and organic traffic.
So yeah, usually like it's pretty immediate.
We usually, I mean, the first thing we watch for is did the content get indexed?
And is it getting impressions?
If it did get indexed, we will submit it for manual indexing.
That could be when content doesn't get indexed automatically, it can be because...

It just wasn't discovered on time, but they could be, sometimes there are technical issues with the site, but usually submitting it for manual indexing gets it indexed, but most of the time it just gets picked up automatically.
And then a couple of, it might take like a week or two to start seeing some clicks, but usually the impact is pretty quick.
Cool.
Okay, nice.
All right.
It's exciting, it's starting to see some real progress.
It's great.
Yeah, perfect.
Yeah, I'm excited too.
I think we just started publishing like two or three weeks ago for a client and we're already seeing like clicks and rankings and that's always like exciting no matter how many times I do it.
So it's nice to see that it works.
Yeah, and then so actually for next steps, we have a piece of content that's relatively finalized.
I would need to just go through and approve that also in the Airtable just to clarify and then.
Okay.
Confirm that you guys do have access now to the CMS and you'll start building it there?
Or are we waiting on that?
Because we're also in this point now where we are doing that kind of switchover between two blog instances.
So I just want to make sure that we're not stepping on any toes with our team when they're attempting to move things over.
Yeah, so I think the next step is if our calibration blog is ready to publish, you can just drop a comment in the doc and just tag Bailey and let her know it's ready for publishing.
And then our team, they can stage it on your blog, and if you feel comfortable, then you can push it live.
Perfect.
Yeah.
Okay.
All right.
Perfect.
Thank you so much for your time.
We'll see you next week.
Yep.
All right.
Thanks a lot.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Weekly sync between Relay Financial and GrowthX to discuss content strategy, performance, and next steps.](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=11.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Content production is ramping up to 5 articles/week; Relay team to approve more topics](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=612.0)
  - [Relay prefers in-house design for blog images; stand-in images acceptable for now](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=810.0)
  - [New Looker dashboard provides detailed performance metrics, including LLM referrals](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=909.0)
  - [Initial content results expected within 1-2 weeks; significant impact around 2-month mark](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1458.0)

## Topics

### Content Production Update

  - [Calibration blog revised with addressed comments](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=358.0)
  - [First of three new blogs for the week completed and sent for review](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=363.0)
  - [Ramping up to 5 articles/week; need more approved topics from Relay team](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=612.0)
  - [Balance needed across content clusters, considering keyword volume and difficulty](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=663.0)

### Blog Image Strategy

  - [GrowthX presented AI-generated featured images for blog posts](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=734.0)
  - [Relay prefers using in-house design team for consistency](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=780.0)
  - [Agreed on using stand-in images to avoid publishing delays](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=843.0)
  - [Relay to establish a process with their design team for image creation](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=855.0)

### Performance Dashboard Introduction

  - [New Looker dashboard provides comprehensive analytics from GA4 and GSC](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=909.0)
  - [Highlights include high engaged sessions ratio and LLM referral breakdown](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=953.0)
  - [Dashboard updates in near real-time and will track GrowthX content performance](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1412.0)
  - [Useful for topic ideation and tracking individual cluster performance](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1161.0)

### Content Performance Expectations

  - [Initial signals (impressions, clicks) typically appear within a week](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1458.0)
  - [Significant impact on organic traffic and rankings expected around 2-month mark](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1469.0)
  - [Content indexing monitored; manual submission if not indexed automatically](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1482.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Dave to review and approve finalized content piece in Airtable](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1554.0)
  - [Relay team to drop comment in calibration blog doc when ready for publishing](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1579.0)
  - [GrowthX team to stage approved content on Relay's blog for final review](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1591.0)
  - [Relay to work on establishing a process for blog image creation with their design team](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=855.0)
  - [Continue monitoring Looker dashboard for early performance signals](https://fathom.video/share/WrPniwRivf8gu6uZyhpdm3bWytdW519f?tab=summary&timestamp=1602.0)

action_items.description: Review & approve content in Airtable for next batch of articles
Discuss w/ design team process for creating blog featured images
Coordinate w/ Carolyn & Polly re: funnel for blog featured images
Review Looker dashboard, note any questions
Approve calibration blog post, tag Bailey in doc when ready to publish
